---

Before we get into the details of the forces that shape habits, I want to talk about an important concept that is best illustrated with the story. And that's the story of Southwest Airlines. Throughout the 1970s, Southwest grew rapidly when most of the airline industry was going bankrupt. Southwest was posting a profit each year up until the point of about 1995, 1996, when they had offers from over 100 cities to grow and expand to their area. Now, if you think about this from a business standpoint, this is a great problem to have, right? You have over 100 cities that are asking for you to expand. Most businesses would say, take advantage, right? Like grow while you can. You don't know what the future is going to look like. Southwest, however, did something different. They looked at the options and then they decided to only expand to four new cities over the next year. And one of the reasons that Southwest leadership decided to do this is because they wanted to stick with a pace that they could sustain. They had grown steadily over the last 20 or 25 years and they knew that they could expand to four cities while still maintaining the same level of quality and service. And so they decided to turn down 95% of offers rather than running and going at every opportunity. Now, this is interesting if we think about it through the lens of habits and behavior change because so often we set goals and habits for ourselves and we try to achieve the maximum amount possible. We try to get to as much as we possibly can on any given day. The implicit assumption whenever we set a goal is, well, here's the minimum I want to do. I want to earn at least 100,000 over the next six months. I want to lose at least 20 pounds in the next five months. I want to do at least 20 push-ups today. Whatever the milestone may be, we have some minimum threshold that we want to get to. And then if we can go beyond that, the assumption is, well, of course, you should take advantage. But instead, I think there's a more useful way to think about our habits and routines. And that is to set an upper bound for our behavior. I have a reader who over the course of five or six weeks wanted to build an exercise habit. And instead of trying to bite off more than he could chew or getting really motivated and going to the gym as much as possible, he decided, I'm going to go to the gym each day, but I'm not going to stay for more than five minutes. And once I get to five minutes, then I'm going to leave. And the interesting thing that happened was that over the course of those four or five, six weeks, he started showing up at the gym and he was like, I'm here all the time. I kind of feel like staying longer. And that's the complete opposite of what most people do when it comes to building a new habit, right? He focused on building the habit first on showing up consistently. And then once he showed up in a routine fashion, then he focused on improving and optimizing. And this is the key insight about repetition and doing a little bit less than what you think you're capable of each time. And that is that you maintain standardization and consistency because repetition itself can be a form of change. Repetition of what you can already do, what you already know you can achieve is a form of change itself by simply maintaining more consistency than you have before. And you have to standardize the process first before you can optimize it. There can be no optimization without standardization. And so with this in mind, I'd like you to focus on setting an upper bound for your behavior rather than a lower bound. So for example, I want to make at least 10 free throws today but not more than 15. I want to lose at least five pounds over the next three months but not more than 10. By setting this kind of upper limit for yourself, you provide the opportunity to not only show up on days when you feel good, but also show up on days when you feel bad because you're simply focusing on showing up and building the behavior rather than on achieving the most possible.
